author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: One of my favorite salads for years and I always order it when I find it in a resto. However, a few years ago I dropped the breading and frying of the chevre. Instead, I simply heat part of a log of goat cheese in the oven or microwave with olive oil and fines herbs. Then I plunk the warm soft chevre on my dressed greens and eat with crusty bread. For me, it's the same or even better and reminds me of my years living in Paris.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: A recipe that comes with such an interesting and well written background story behind it makes it all the more enticing. I'm looking forward to making this soon. The dental floss is a practical touch as a stand in for the wire often used at cheese counters, and I laughed at "unflavored" as I entertained (and subsequently dismissed) the idea of using mint flavored floss!

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: Great story about why we travel and how the history and culture of a Place can be found on the plate and in the glass. One of the reasons I love New York City is the availability of foods and drink that can allow me to recreate a meal that I enjoyed elsewhere in the world and of course good writing also does this. Thank you for both the article and the recipe.

“In the maze of the old quarter, we sat at a table outside, on stone stairs descending from another century.” 

I am rarely in the kitchen as it is my husband’s domain, however, a version of this recipe has long been my contribution to our dinner parties.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: Make salad dressing in mostly empty dijon mustard jar, or with the dregs of the mustard in the jar. Eyeball the oil & vinegar, salt & pepper, whatever extra herbs (fine or not), give a good shake. Oh, and a pinch of sugar (gasp!!!) Extra keeps in fridge for weeks-just keep adding to it.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: I love this story. It reminds me of when I was young and traveling on my own, always with low funds. I have made this recipe for many years, and guests are always amazed at how delicious it is, yet so simple. I've never used the egg mixture, but it makes sense...and after patting them with the bread crumbs, I put the little cheese rounds in a toaster oven...my go-to kitchen appliance. And thanks for the idea of sherry vinegar instead of red wine vinegar, which I don't use much any more.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: I first had this salad years ago at Alice Waters' Berkeley restaurant Cafe Fanny.  We've been making it at home ever since, a version very close to this recipe, even down to the dental floss.
readable_ai_guess: Liked recipe and prepared as written

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: "In the maze of the old quarter, we sat at a table outside, on stone stairs descending from another century."  Yes, she got me there, too. Just happens I have fresh fromage from the chèvre at hand, market greens, shallots, and a glass of red reminding me of those sunlit evenings in France, Italy, and Spain ... and the people who shared laughter, wisdom, and so many stories. I raise my glass to them, in quiet, wistful gratitude. Merci, Grazie, Gracias!

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: Nothing takes me back to the joys of eating in France like le salade de chevre chaud. I usually just make little chevre toasts in the microwave, but this is a fabulous alternative.  I usually favor white wine vinegar, and add egg and/or walnuts for my own trip down memory lane.  Thanks so much!

author: Ligaya Mishan

commentBody: Such a lovely essay to go with a favorite salad. Can't wait to try this home version.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: I've had (and loved) various salades de chèvre chaud around France and even Quebec. But when I try to recreate, I feel as if the chèvre here in the U.S. just isn't the same. I've tried goat brie, which is closer but still not a match. Any suggestions?

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: This is one of my favorite salads- but the first I have heard of frying breaded goat cheese.  I will try it out.  The more standard preparation is to smear the goat cheese on small pieces of toasted soft bread (not baguette).  There are other variations.  One I enjoyed in Paris had the goat cheese on toast pieces with pesto and honey.  Also, there is no rule that the salad has to be only greens.  Add what you like.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this other NYT version of the salad by Alice Waters? https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1012960-baked-goat-cheese-with-garden-lettuces?smid=ck-recipe-iOS-share

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: Variation for lazy cooks (and the one I grew up with in France): toast good bread, add one or two slices of chèvre onto the bread, a sprinkle of herbes de provence and toss under the broiler for a few minutes, serve on top of the greens.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: Answering that question about "fine herbs". The term is actually French: "fines herbes". Here's an explanation: https://www.thespruceeats.com/what-are-fines-herbes-995671#:~:text=Fines herbes is defined as,be found in fines herbes.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: I've never made a salad like this before with warm goat cheese rounds, so I followed the recipe as is and it was simple and delicious - as promised. The dressing is superb and one I expect I'll be making often. The goat cheese rounds turned out browned and yummy (I used some chopped thyme in the breading as suggested). I cooked them for 2 minutes on each side. I might cook them a little longer next time so they get a little more gooey. A winner.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody:
This is one of my favorite salads—I taught myself how to make it after first having it in NYC during college! There’s a version @ local haunt here in LA that bulks it up with wild rice—have made that version too, and even cut back on the cheese (a little) & added grilled tofu (I know, eye roll), but these are excellent ways to bulk it up if you’re skipping the bread. 

That being said, I’m going to get back to basics and make this exactly as written as fast as you can say “Oui!”

commentBody: Lala - Great idea! I too lived in France and ate copious quantities of Salade de Chevre Chaud, but I can no longer tolerate eggs or wheat so I've resorted to just crumbling chevre over my salads, but your idea offers another alternative. Love it.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: In our house and in some of my friend’s homes, no nearly empty jar of Dijon mustard gets tossed.  Never measure, eyeball good olive oil, and either sherry or balsamic vinegar, a chopped garlic clove or shallot. A good shake is all that is needed. By the way, this salad is the first salad I ever ordered when I ate out the first time when I lived in Bruxelles years ago. I do remember that salad had some herbes de Provence and honey in it.
